old_claim.note: Machine Learning (Early Algorithms) is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim_absent: Platform rules and human enforcement workflows existed before automated classifiers and do not require machine learning.

source_shape.source_locator: The history section describes moderation in early virtual communities and a major shift during the 2000s and early 2010s as large private platforms intensified intervention and expanded governance structures.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The platform-moderation history describes early community enforcement and later 2000s governance expansion without making machine learning constitutive.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The documented moderation lineage begins with policy, users, and platform intervention, placing classifiers as an optional later scaling technique rather than an origin prerequisite.

ontology_before: All social-media content moderation was modeled as descending from early machine-learning algorithms.
ontology_after: Platform moderation depends on social platforms; automated classification can remain a later implementation layer.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-12-launch-source-check-completion: social_media_content_moderation no longer directly depends on machine_learning_early_algorithms.
would_reject_if: The node is narrowed specifically to automated content classification.
Evidence shows the earliest scoped platform moderation could not operate without machine learning.
short_reason: Platform rules and human enforcement workflows existed before automated classifiers and do not require machine learning.
